questions.questionText: The parotid gland is traversed by which nerve?
questions.explanation: The facial nerve (CN VII) traverses the parotid gland, dividing it into superficial and deep lobes. Within the gland it divides into 5 terminal branches: Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal mandibular, Cervical (mnemonic: 'To Zanzibar By Motor Car'). Parotid surgery risks facial nerve damage. The nerve does NOT supply the gland (auriculotemporal nerve provides secretomotor). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Parotid Region.

questions.questionText: The branch of the facial nerve most vulnerable during parotid surgery is:
questions.explanation: The marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve is most vulnerable during parotid surgery because it courses superficially, often below the mandible (75% of cases, it dips 1-2 cm below). Damage causes drooping of the angle of mouth (inability to depress lower lip). The buccal branch has cross-innervation, so damage is less clinically significant. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Facial Nerve Branches.

questions.questionText: Frey's syndrome occurs after parotid surgery due to aberrant regeneration of:
questions.explanation: Frey's syndrome (gustatory sweating) occurs when parasympathetic secretomotor fibers of the auriculotemporal nerve (meant for the parotid gland) aberrantly regenerate and innervate sweat glands in the overlying skin. This causes sweating and flushing over the parotid region during eating. Treatment: tympanic neurectomy or botulinum toxin injection. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Auriculotemporal Nerve.

questions.questionText: The carotid body is a:
questions.explanation: The carotid body (at the carotid bifurcation) is a peripheral chemoreceptor that detects changes in PaO2, PaCO2, and pH. It is supplied by CN IX (glossopharyngeal nerve). The carotid SINUS (slight dilation at the beginning of internal carotid) is a baroreceptor (also CN IX). Carotid body tumor = chemodectoma (paraganglioma). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Carotid System.

questions.questionText: The internal carotid artery does NOT give any branch in the:
questions.explanation: The internal carotid artery gives NO branches in the neck (cervical part). Its branches begin only after it enters the skull. Branches of the cerebral part include ophthalmic artery, posterior communicating artery, anterior choroidal artery, and terminal bifurcation (ACA, MCA). The external carotid artery gives 8 branches in the neck. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Internal Carotid Artery.

questions.questionText: The thyroid gland is supplied by the superior thyroid artery, which is a branch of:
questions.explanation: Superior thyroid artery = FIRST branch of the external carotid artery. It is closely related to the external laryngeal nerve (injury during thyroidectomy causes voice change — loss of cricothyroid function). Inferior thyroid artery arises from the thyrocervical trunk (subclavian) and is related to the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Thyroidea ima artery (if present) arises from the brachiocephalic trunk. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thyroid Blood Supply.

questions.questionText: During thyroidectomy, the nerve at risk at the ligament of Berry (close to the capsule of the thyroid) is:
questions.explanation: The recurrent laryngeal nerve (branch of vagus) runs in the tracheoesophageal groove and enters the larynx behind the cricothyroid joint, closely related to the ligament of Berry (posterior suspensory ligament of thyroid). It is at greatest risk during thyroidectomy near the inferior thyroid artery and Berry's ligament. Unilateral RLN injury causes hoarseness; bilateral causes stridor/airway obstruction. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thyroid Surgery.

questions.questionText: The external laryngeal nerve supplies which muscle?
questions.explanation: External laryngeal nerve (branch of superior laryngeal nerve, from vagus) supplies the cricothyroid muscle ONLY (tensor of vocal cords). It runs with the superior thyroid artery and is at risk during ligation of this artery. Damage causes a monotone/weak voice (inability to tense or elongate vocal cords). All other intrinsic laryngeal muscles are supplied by the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Laryngeal Nerves.

questions.questionText: The only abductor of the vocal cords is:
questions.explanation: Posterior cricoarytenoid is the ONLY abductor (opener) of the vocal cords. All other intrinsic laryngeal muscles are adductors/tensors. Bilateral RLN palsy → both cords in paramedian/adducted position → stridor (as posterior cricoarytenoid is the first to be affected). Memory aid: 'PCA is the only abductor — PCA Opens the cords.' Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Laryngeal Muscles.

questions.questionText: The sensory nerve supply of the anterior 2/3 of the tongue (general sensation) is from:
questions.explanation: Anterior 2/3 of tongue: general sensation → lingual nerve (V3, mandibular division of trigeminal); taste → chorda tympani (VII, facial nerve, carried by lingual nerve). Posterior 1/3 of tongue: both general sensation AND taste → glossopharyngeal nerve (IX). Hypoglossal nerve (XII) is purely motor (supplies all tongue muscles except palatoglossus). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Tongue Innervation.

questions.questionText: The muscle that protrudes the tongue is:
questions.explanation: Genioglossus protrudes the tongue (pushes it forward). It is supplied by the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII). In hypoglossal nerve palsy, the tongue deviates toward the affected side on protrusion (due to unopposed action of the opposite genioglossus). In unconscious patients, genioglossus relaxation causes tongue to fall back, obstructing the airway. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Tongue Muscles.

questions.questionText: The submandibular duct (Wharton's duct) opens at the:
questions.optionB: Sublingual papilla on the floor of the mouth
questions.explanation: Wharton's duct (submandibular duct) opens at the sublingual papilla (caruncla sublingualis) on the floor of the mouth, beside the frenulum of the tongue. The parotid duct (Stensen's duct) opens opposite the upper second molar tooth. The sublingual gland opens via multiple ducts (of Rivinus) along the sublingual fold. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Salivary Glands.

questions.questionText: The nerve at risk during submandibular gland excision is:
questions.explanation: During submandibular gland (SMG) excision: (1) Marginal mandibular branch of facial nerve crosses superficially — can cause drooping of mouth angle, (2) Lingual nerve loops under Wharton's duct ('the lingual nerve took a curve around the Wharton's duct' — it passes lateral, then inferior, then medial to the duct) — damage causes loss of sensation and taste (anterior 2/3, via chorda tympani). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Submandibular Region.

questions.questionText: The most common site of a branchial cyst is:
questions.explanation: Second branchial cleft remnants are the most common (95% of branchial cysts). They present as a cystic swelling along the anterior border of sternocleidomastoid at the junction of its upper and middle thirds. If a fistula forms, external opening is along the anterior SCM border, internal opening is in the tonsillar fossa. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Pharyngeal Apparatus Development.

questions.questionText: The foramen rotundum transmits the:
questions.explanation: Foramina and contents: Superior orbital fissure → V1 (ophthalmic), CN III, IV, VI, ophthalmic veins. Foramen rotundum → V2 (maxillary). Foramen ovale → V3 (mandibular), accessory meningeal artery. Foramen spinosum → middle meningeal artery, meningeal branch of V3. Memory aid: 'Standing Room Only' = Superior orbital fissure (V1), Rotundum (V2), Ovale (V3). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Skull Base Foramina.

questions.questionText: The middle meningeal artery enters the skull through which foramen?
questions.explanation: Middle meningeal artery (branch of maxillary artery, first part) enters the skull through foramen spinosum. It is the most common source of epidural (extradural) hematoma, typically from fracture of the pterion (thinnest part of skull, where anterior branch runs). Epidural = lens-shaped/biconvex on CT, does NOT cross suture lines. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Meningeal Arteries.

questions.questionText: The weakest point of the skull (pterion) overlies which artery?
questions.explanation: Pterion = junction of frontal, parietal, temporal, and greater wing of sphenoid bones. It is the thinnest part of the skull and overlies the anterior branch of the middle meningeal artery. Trauma here causes epidural hematoma — patient has a 'lucid interval' followed by rapid deterioration. Emergency: burr hole at the pterion. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Skull Lateral Aspect.

questions.questionText: The nerve that supplies the muscles of mastication is:
questions.explanation: Mandibular nerve (V3) supplies the muscles of mastication: masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid, lateral pterygoid (plus mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, tensor veli palatini, tensor tympani). It is the only division of the trigeminal nerve containing both sensory and motor fibers. V1 and V2 are purely sensory. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Mandibular Nerve.

questions.questionText: The cavernous sinus contains which cranial nerves in its lateral wall?
questions.optionB: CN III, IV, V1, V2, VI
questions.explanation: Cavernous sinus lateral wall (from superior to inferior): CN III (oculomotor), CN IV (trochlear), CN V1 (ophthalmic), CN V2 (maxillary). WITHIN the sinus (passing through it, not in the wall): CN VI (abducens) and Internal Carotid Artery. CN VI is most commonly affected in cavernous sinus thrombosis (medial position). EXAM TRAP: VI is INSIDE, not in the wall. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cavernous Sinus.

questions.questionText: The dangerous area of the face refers to the area drained by the facial vein because it communicates with the cavernous sinus via the:
questions.explanation: The 'dangerous triangle of the face' (bounded by nose bridge and both mouth angles) drains via the facial vein → deep facial vein → pterygoid venous plexus → emissary veins → cavernous sinus, AND via the angular vein → superior ophthalmic vein → cavernous sinus. Infections can spread to the cavernous sinus (cavernous sinus thrombosis) — potentially fatal. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Face Veins.

questions.questionText: The maxillary artery is a terminal branch of the external carotid artery. Its most important branch is the:
questions.explanation: The sphenopalatine artery (terminal branch of maxillary artery, 3rd part) is the most important artery of epistaxis — it supplies the nasal septum and lateral nasal wall. It enters the nose through the sphenopalatine foramen. For posterior epistaxis, sphenopalatine artery ligation is performed. Kiesselbach's plexus (Little's area) on the anterior septum is the commonest overall site of epistaxis. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Maxillary Artery.

questions.questionText: The nerve that carries taste from the anterior 2/3 of the tongue is:
questions.explanation: Chorda tympani (branch of facial nerve CN VII) carries taste from anterior 2/3 of tongue AND parasympathetic secretomotor fibers to submandibular and sublingual glands. It joins the lingual nerve in the infratemporal fossa. The lingual nerve itself carries general sensation (touch, pain, temperature) only. Greater petrosal nerve carries taste from palate. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Facial Nerve Branches.

questions.questionText: The muscle that opens the jaw (depresses the mandible) is:
questions.explanation: Lateral pterygoid (inferior head) is the prime mover for jaw opening (depression). It also protrudes the mandible and, acting unilaterally, produces lateral movements (side-to-side chewing). Jaw closure (elevation) is by masseter, temporalis, and medial pterygoid. Gravity also assists jaw opening, but lateral pterygoid is the active opener. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — TMJ Movements.

questions.questionText: The inferior thyroid artery is closely related to which nerve?
questions.explanation: The inferior thyroid artery (from thyrocervical trunk) has an intimate and variable relationship with the recurrent laryngeal nerve at the level of the lower pole of the thyroid gland. The nerve may pass anterior, posterior, or between the branches of the artery. This relationship is the most common site of RLN injury during thyroidectomy. Surgeons identify the nerve before ligating the artery. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thyroid Surgery Anatomy.

questions.questionText: Which cranial nerve exits from the dorsal aspect of the brainstem?
questions.explanation: The trochlear nerve (CN IV) is the ONLY cranial nerve that exits from the dorsal (posterior) aspect of the brainstem (below the inferior colliculus). It is also: the thinnest CN, has the longest intracranial course, the only CN to completely decussate, and supplies only one muscle (superior oblique). These features make it vulnerable to head trauma. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cranial Nerves.

questions.questionText: The jugular foramen transmits all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Jugular foramen transmits: CN IX (glossopharyngeal), CN X (vagus), CN XI (spinal accessory), internal jugular vein, inferior petrosal sinus, posterior meningeal artery. CN XII (hypoglossal) passes through the hypoglossal canal. Memory aid: 'Jugular = 9, 10, 11; Hypoglossal canal = 12.' Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Skull Base Foramina.

questions.questionText: A patient presents with deviation of the uvula to the RIGHT side. The nerve damaged is:
questions.explanation: Uvula deviates AWAY from the side of the lesion (opposite to tongue deviation). If uvula deviates to the RIGHT → LEFT vagus nerve palsy (paralysis of left palatine muscles → right-sided muscles pull the uvula to the right). In tongue deviation: tongue deviates TOWARD the side of hypoglossal nerve lesion (due to unopposed contralateral genioglossus). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Vagus Nerve.

questions.questionText: The lymph from the tip of the tongue drains primarily into the:
questions.explanation: Lymphatic drainage of the tongue: Tip → submental nodes (BILATERAL drainage — hence tip of tongue cancer has bilateral nodal metastasis). Anterior 2/3 (lateral) → ipsilateral submandibular → deep cervical nodes. Posterior 1/3 → bilateral deep cervical nodes (jugulo-digastric node = 'tonsillar node'). Bilateral drainage of tip and posterior 1/3 is clinically important. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Tongue Lymphatics.

questions.questionText: The muscle supplied by the spinal part of the accessory nerve (CN XI) is:
questions.explanation: Spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) supplies sternocleidomastoid (SCM) and trapezius. SCM: turns head to opposite side, flexes neck. Trapezius: shrugs shoulders, retracts and rotates scapula. Injury (posterior triangle neck surgery, lymph node biopsy) causes inability to shrug shoulder (trapezius palsy) and weakness turning head. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Accessory Nerve.

questions.questionText: The blood supply to the parathyroid glands is mainly from the:
questions.explanation: Both superior and inferior parathyroid glands receive their main blood supply from the inferior thyroid artery. During thyroidectomy, preserving the inferior thyroid artery branches close to the thyroid capsule helps maintain parathyroid blood supply and prevent hypoparathyroidism (hypocalcemia). The most common cause of hypoparathyroidism is inadvertent removal/devascularization during thyroid surgery. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Parathyroid Glands.
